title: Reducing washing machine water use
subtitle: Full loads, correct programme selection, and cold-wash settings
excerpt: A modern A-rated washing machine uses 45-55 litres per cycle. An older C or D-rated machine uses 70-100 litres. The difference is the machine, but programme choice, load size, and temperature setting also cut consumption by 20-30 percent without replacing the appliance.
sourceNotes: WRAP Water in Washing research (2021) on UK domestic laundry water consumption. Energy Saving Trust washing machine efficiency guidance (2024). DECC/BEIS consumer appliance water use dataset.

content.text: A washing machine is one of the highest water users in the home after the toilet and shower. UK households run an average of 4-5 cycles per week. Optimising those cycles for water reduces consumption without any capital outlay.

A washing machine uses approximately the same volume of water regardless of whether the drum is half full or full. A half-load cycle uses the same water as a full cycle but washes half the clothes. Running full loads consistently reduces the number of cycles per week and halves the water used per kilogram of laundry cleaned.

Most machines rate their capacity by dry laundry weight. A 7 kg machine is designed to wash up to 7 kg of dry clothes. Fill the drum without compressing the load: clothes should turn freely. A drum packed so tightly the clothes cannot move cleans poorly and uses the full water allocation without better results.

Eco programmes on most machines are optimised for water efficiency: they use less water per cycle at the cost of a longer run time. The extra time allows the machine to achieve equivalent cleaning with less water. Most lightly soiled everyday laundry can use the eco programme without any loss of cleaning performance.

Quick-wash programmes are short but are not water-efficient. They often use as much water as a standard cycle. On eco programmes, the water saving comes from longer soak time and lower temperature, not from a shorter cycle.

Temperature does not directly affect water volume, but it does affect energy consumption per litre. Cold-wash cycles at 20 degrees C use 40-60 percent less energy per cycle than a 40 degree wash. Modern detergents labelled as suitable for 30 degrees C or cold wash perform adequately on everyday clothing at 20-30 degrees C. Reserve 60 degree washes for heavily soiled items, nappies, or bed linen where pathogen reduction is a priority.

A machine rated D or below uses significantly more water and energy per cycle than a modern A-rated model. The break-even for replacement is roughly seven to ten years of use at the higher rate. If an older machine also needs repair, an A-rated replacement is usually the better choice.

Pre-soaking heavily soiled items in a bucket for 30-60 minutes before washing reduces the need for multiple cycles or high-temperature cycles. The pre-soak water can be added to a compost heap (if it contains no detergent) or to a planted bed (if it contains a small amount of standard detergent at low concentration). This is not necessary for lightly soiled everyday laundry.
